evals.prompt: Write a function `binary_search(arr, target)` in Python that returns the index of target in a sorted array, or -1 if not found. Use formal pre/post conditions and derive the implementation from the specification.
evals.expected_output: Formal Pre/Post specification, derivation of implementation from postcondition analysis, correct implementation, and test cases derived from the specification structure

assertions.text: States a formal precondition (Pre) for the function — e.g., arr is sorted, arr is a list of comparable elements
States a formal postcondition (Post) relating inputs and output — e.g., result = i ↔ arr[i] = target; result = -1 ↔ target ∉ arr
Explains how the implementation is derived from the postcondition (not just written ad-hoc)
Derives test cases from the postcondition structure (target found, target not found, edge cases from precondition boundaries)
The binary search implementation is correct and handles edge cases (empty array, target at boundaries)

evals.prompt: Implement a partition function for quicksort in Python: `partition(arr, lo, hi)` that rearranges arr[lo..hi] around a pivot and returns the pivot index. Prove the loop invariant and termination.
evals.expected_output: Loop invariant stated formally, proof that body maintains invariant, bounding function for termination, correct implementation in Python

assertions.text: States the loop invariant explicitly (e.g., all elements in arr[lo..i] ≤ pivot and arr[j..hi] ≥ pivot)
Proves (or argues) that the loop body maintains the invariant
States a bounding function (variant) that decreases at each iteration and proves the loop terminates
The partition implementation is correct and works with quicksort
Implementation is in Python as requested

evals.expected_output: Inductive proof: base case (len ≤ 1), inductive step (assuming merge_sort correct on halves → correct on whole), termination proof, test cases derived from proof structure

assertions.text: Proves base case: arr with 0 or 1 elements is already sorted (merge_sort returns it correctly)
States and uses the inductive hypothesis: assumes merge_sort is correct on arrays smaller than n, proves it is correct on arrays of size n
Proves termination: len(arr) strictly decreases at each recursive call, reaching the base case
Either identifies the bug in merge (left[i:] + right[j:] append is correct, but checks merge preserves sorted order) or confirms both functions are correct
Derives test cases from proof structure: base case (empty, single), recursive case (two elements, general), merge correctness (equal elements, already sorted halves)
